critique.summary: The article describes a method called 'Font Smuggler' that allows users to copy and paste locked custom brand fonts into Google Docs and Slides without permission. It highlights a loophole in Google Workspace's font embedding system, enabling unauthorized use of paid custom corporate fonts via copy-paste, though it does not work on Google mobile apps.
mainPoints: Google Workspace allows brands to embed custom corporate fonts into their documents and slides if they pay for them.
These custom fonts are normally locked to the paying brands and not accessible to others.
A loophole exists where users can copy and paste these locked fonts into Google Docs or Slides to use them without permission.
The method does not work on Google mobile applications.
critique.truthfulness: The claim that Google Workspace allows embedding of custom fonts for paying brands is accurate and documented by Google. However, the assertion that users can copy and paste these locked fonts into Google Docs to use them without permission exploits a technical loophole that may or may not be consistently effective, and Google may patch such vulnerabilities. There is no official endorsement or documentation from Google supporting this method, and using it likely violates Google Workspace's terms of service and intellectual property rights. The article does not cite any sources or provide evidence beyond the author's description, so the truthfulness of the exploit's reliability and legality is uncertain.
considerations: Using locked brand fonts without permission may violate copyright and licensing agreements.
Google may update its software to close this loophole, making the method obsolete.
The article does not discuss potential legal or ethical implications of using this exploit.
No technical details or evidence are provided to verify the exploit's effectiveness.
The article lacks citations or perspectives from Google or affected brands.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the challenges and nuances of using custom brand fonts in Google Docs and presentations. Commenters share experiences with font licensing, brand identity, and the technical aspects of font usage and access.

comments.author: jmathai
comments.text:
Received DMCA takedown notices for a paid font I used for my wife’s interior design website that she liked but we didn’t pay for because…I’m lazy.

I was surprised to receive the DMCA (it is hosted on GitHub Pages). I ignored the emails because…I’m lazy.

They (GitHub) eventually took down the repository (and site). So I swapped to another font and I don’t think my wife noticed.

I think all of this was still easier than probably paying for the font!

Lesson of the story? Don’t underestimate the impact of laziness on your potential customers.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a real-world example of font licensing enforcement and the consequences of using unlicensed fonts, illustrating the importance of respecting font licenses.

comments.author: dijit
comments.text:
Whoa. How does this work?

One of the major issues we had at my previous company weaning people off of powerpoint (to google docs) was brand fonts. Ours, of course.

A lot of what is considered brand identity in presentations comes from fonts, which makes Google Docs Slides a non-starter for many unfortunately.

(we ended up making them in powerpoint and using the Google Docs compatibility mode with pptx).
comments.analysis: This comment insightfully highlights the practical challenges companies face when transitioning presentation tools due to brand font restrictions.

comments.author: ngrilly
comments.text: Would be really good if Google Docs could support custom brand fonts by letting their customers upload them in the admin console.
comments.analysis: A constructive suggestion that aligns with common enterprise needs, reflecting a realistic feature request for Google Docs.

comments.author: WillAdams
comments.text: Surprised that such font access isn't gated by IP address --- usually font licenses are quite restrictive and have such requirements for usage.
comments.analysis: This comment correctly points out typical licensing restrictions and raises a valid question about how fonts are accessed in this context.

comments.author: tavavex
comments.text: I didn't know that many brands had their own bespoke fonts. Especially the less prominent players like Colgate or Korean Air. Was this caused by normal font licensing being so restrictive or expensive that they just decided to hire someone to make a font just for them, or design teams insisting that this sans serif that looks almost like all the others (but not quite) is essential to their design?
comments.analysis: This comment reflects genuine curiosity about brand font practices and licensing, which is a reasonable observation though somewhat speculative.

comments.author: ILoveHorses
comments.text: Any idea how did the creator manage to get access to the fonts in the first place? Won't you need a Google Docs document which uses the given font and then copy it from there and put it up on the website? Or is there some way the creator could have put these fonts on his website from publicly available information?
comments.analysis: A reasonable technical inquiry about the method used to extract fonts, showing engagement with the technical details of the project.

comments.author: virtualritz
comments.text:
I love how all these 'brand' fonts look indistinguishable to an untrained eye and still brain-frying-bordedom-inducingly close to each other to someone like me who actually studied & worked in typography.

Related: https://eidosdesign.substack.com/p/why-every-brand-looks-the...
comments.analysis: An insightful comment from a typography professional that adds depth to the discussion about brand font design and perception.

comments.author: novov
comments.text: Is there any difference between Source Serif and Source Sans as listed here and the publicly available versions, given they are open source typefaces?
comments.analysis: A relevant question about font versions that shows attention to detail but does not provide a definitive answer.

comments.author: varun_ch
comments.text: I knew about this for Google’s own fonts but had no idea they offered the option to use custom fonts. Is there any easy place to find a list of them? I wonder if the custom fonts are just hardcoded/pushed to their CDN alongside all the other ones.
comments.analysis: This comment expresses curiosity about Google Fonts offerings, contributing to the discussion but without new factual information.

comments.author: love2read
comments.text: I really like the style of copying the “google tool” style that this website and jmail use. It makes the project feel different compared to all the ai-generated app these days.
comments.analysis: This comment is mostly an opinion on design style and does not add substantive information to the discussion.

comments.author: youniverse
comments.text: I was just looking into the custom anthropic sans and serif fonts used for the claude app, I really like those!
comments.analysis: A personal preference comment that does not contribute to the technical or licensing discussion.

comments.author: numbers
comments.text: because they're brand fonts, none of them feel great to write more than a headline with
comments.analysis: A subjective observation about brand fonts' usability that is plausible but not deeply informative.
